reflection.error_identification: No user feedback was provided, so there are no user-prioritized blocker issues to carry forward.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: N/A (no user-reported symptoms/requests).
reflection.correct_approach: Proceed with blueprint and metric-driven diagnosis only; consider adding a short auto-generated “what changed / what to verify” checklist in outputs for easier future user feedback.
reflection.key_insight: When feedback is absent, treat metrics + blueprint compliance as the primary acceptance gates and improve observability so users can give targeted feedback next iteration.

blueprint_refs: validation requires: "Only keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days within the 2021 subset (d-7..d-1) available"

evidence.metrics: validation_set.num_users=1 num_user_days=11; excluded_val_missing_context=681

reflection.error_identification: Evaluation is effectively computed on a degenerate validation set (11 days for 1 user), making metrics unstable and unrepresentative.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The blueprint requires using the past 7 observed days (t-7..t-1) within 2021 for Motivation, but CURRENT CODE interprets this as requiring the prior 7 calendar dates to exist in data (contiguous daily coverage). In holdout_split it checks (d-1..d-7) membership in observed_set, which is much stricter than “past 7 observed days.” This excludes most validation candidates (681 excluded). MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation repeats the same contiguous-date requirement by returning defaults unless every one of those 7 calendar dates exists.
reflection.correct_approach:
Align to blueprint phrasing “past 7 observed days”:
- In holdout_split: for each candidate validation date d, instead of checking calendar contiguity, sort that user’s 2021 observed dates < d and require at least 7 prior dates; use the latest 7 of those for Motivation eligibility.
- In _compute_motivation: compute last7 by selecting the most recent 7 observed days strictly before target_day (within 2021), not (target_day - i).
This will expand validation coverage and produce meaningful metrics.
reflection.key_insight: Do not confuse “past N observed days” with “past N calendar days”; using calendar contiguity will collapse datasets and invalidate evaluation.

blueprint_refs: Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 days (t-7..t-1) within 2021 only (no future leakage)
Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date.

evidence.metrics: No direct metric indicates leakage, but evaluation is on a temporal holdout and simulator reads agent.observed_2021 for motivation.

reflection.error_identification: The simulator’s Motivation feature is not guaranteed to be computed strictly from pre-target training data; it can read from the full 2021 observed set.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: ResidentAgent stores observed_2021 as all 2021 trajectories (train + validation). MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation indexes agent.observed_2021 directly. While it only looks at days (d-1..d-7), the structure still violates the blueprint rule “strictly pre-target when generating targets” because the allowed context should be derived from the split, not the full dataset object. Once you fix the contiguous-day bug (previous issue) to use “previous 7 observed days,” leakage risk becomes real unless you pass an explicit per-target context set filtered to < target_day and within 2021.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make Motivation context explicit and split-aware:
- Change generate_day()/rollout() signatures (or pass an additional argument) to provide per-user observed history limited to dates < target day from the 2021-only subset.
- Or store agent.train_days separately and, at simulation time for validation date d, compute last7 from agent.train_days plus any observed days < d that are permitted (blueprint implies observed history, but still strictly pre-target).
- Add an assertion/logging guard: max(history_date) < target_day.
reflection.key_insight: Any feature computed from ‘observed logs’ must be derived from an explicit, split-aware history window to prevent accidental leakage when refactoring selection logic.

blueprint_refs: "category share distributions ... mapped via catto.json where applicable"
"time_of_day_category_profile (global or per-coarse-category)"

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.2982 (high); transition_divergence=0.1905 (high)

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day alignment is poor (tod_jsd_avg ~0.30), and transition behavior diverges; category choice likely mixes incompatible category namespaces.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator computes categories using coarse mapping (to_coarse_category) for category shares and transitions, but the simulator’s time-of-day category profile is computed on fine categories (_compute_global_time_profile uses v.category) and _choose_next_category mixes transition PMF (fine categories from ResidentPattern) with prof (fine categories) yet applies weekend downweighting via `c.lower().startswith('office')` and awareness via coarse mapping inconsistently. If catto.json collapses many fine categories into fewer coarse ones, evaluation is on coarse while generation is on fine, causing distribution and transition mismatch at evaluation time.
reflection.correct_approach:
Unify category space for generation to match evaluation:
- Build time_of_day_category_profile on coarse categories: in _compute_global_time_profile, map v.category through coarse_map.
- Build ResidentPattern.category_pref and transition_pmf on coarse categories (or keep both, but ensure _choose_next_category uses the same space as evaluation).
- Ensure POI selection still uses fine category to query POIs; if using coarse for choice, map coarse->allowed fine categories from poi_catalog to pick a fine category then POI.
This targets tod_jsd_avg and transition_divergence directly.
reflection.key_insight: If evaluation collapses labels (fine->coarse), generation must either operate in that same label space or explicitly model the mapping step; mixing spaces silently damages ToD and transition metrics.

blueprint_refs: "Link POI ids to coordinates using poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json by matching the exact 'Category#id' string."
"trip_distance_wasserstein" computed from consecutive-stop distances using POI coordinates.

evidence.metrics: validation gt_trip_distance_meta.coord_match_rate=0.0897 (7/78 pairs matched), while sim coord_match_rate=0.5522

reflection.error_identification: Ground-truth validation distances are mostly uncomputable due to missing coordinate matches (coord_match_rate ~9%), making trip_distance_wasserstein unreliable and likely inflated/unstable.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: parse_day_record accepts any POI token string found in the day record without validating it exists in the POI catalog. Evaluator._trip_distances then can’t find coordinates (env.distance_km returns None), dropping most GT pairs. This is a pipeline bug because the blueprint’s distance metric assumes joining POI tokens to coordinates; if tokens don’t exist in poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json or have formatting variants, the metric becomes dominated by missingness rather than behavioral mismatch.
reflection.correct_approach:
Filter/normalize POI tokens during parsing:
- In parse_day_record (or immediately after load), optionally discard visits whose poi_token is not in env.poi_catalog (requires env access; if not available at parse time, do a second-pass filter after loading POIs).
- Alternatively, keep them but report and exclude such users/days from distance-based metrics with an explicit threshold (e.g., require coord_match_rate >= X for evaluation), and log top missing tokens.
This will make trip_distance_wasserstein meaningful and comparable between GT and sim.
reflection.key_insight: Distance-based evaluation requires a clean join key; always measure and gate on coordinate match rate, otherwise Wasserstein is evaluating missing-data artifacts.

blueprint_refs: "sample baseline number of stops and start time ..." then generate ordered visits
"If home_poi exists ... use it as start" (initial state), but output should be comparable to observed visit sequences.

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.0; stop_count_kl=2.7548; sample outputs start at Park#... (home inferred from last-visit), increasing synthetic stop counts.

reflection.error_identification: Daily stop counts differ substantially from ground truth (KL ~2.75; mean abs error ~1 stop/day).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: generate_day always adds an initial visit if pat.home_poi is not None. But stop_count is sampled from the empirical distribution of observed day-record visit counts, which likely already includes the first observed stop; adding an extra forced “home” visit shifts the distribution upward. Additionally, home_poi fallback uses most frequent last POI if no Home# exists, which can be a non-home POI (e.g., Park#2588 in sample outputs), making the forced initial stop both incorrect and count-inflating.
reflection.correct_approach:
Make stop_count and forced-start consistent:
- Either: treat “start at home” as an internal state and do NOT automatically append it to the output unless the observed format includes home explicitly for that user; in that case output home.
- Or: if you do append home as a visit, then sample additional stops as (stop_count-1) so the total output count matches the empirical distribution.
- Improve home inference: prefer most frequent first-visit POI or nighttime POI if no Home# tokens, rather than last-visit-only.
This will reduce stop_count_kl/abs error and also help ToD.
reflection.key_insight: When you inject a mandatory event (home/start), you must re-parameterize count models so the observable count distribution stays calibrated.

blueprint_refs: "Motivation ... modulates stop count, time-of-day propensity, routine_strength, exploration_rate, and return-home hazard."

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.3548 (low); trip_distance_wasserstein=3.265; tod_jsd_avg=0.298

reflection.error_identification: The model under-recovers frequent POIs (recall ~0.35) and has notable distance/ToD mismatch; Motivation conditioning is only partially implemented.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _compute_motivation produces exploration_delta but generate_day/_choose_next_poi do not apply it; exploration_rate is constant. Similarly, time-of-day propensity is not modulated by Motivation beyond routine_strength mixing; no explicit modifier to the time profile is applied. This violates the blueprint conditioning requirements and reduces the model’s ability to adapt per-day, harming POI recall and temporal realism.
reflection.correct_approach:
Wire Motivation into POI choice and ToD/category choice:
- In _choose_next_poi: set exploration = clip(base_exploration + motivation['exploration_delta'], 0, 1).
- In _choose_next_category: optionally scale prof weights by a Motivation time-shift or “activity propensity” scalar (e.g., stop_multiplier can also increase/decrease prof concentration).
- Consider making return-home hazard depend on Motivation more directly (beyond bias delta), e.g., hazard increases with low stop_multiplier.
This should improve topk_poi_recall and reduce tod_jsd_avg.
reflection.key_insight: If the spec says a conditioning signal modulates parameters, ensure every referenced knob is actually used in the policy; unused computed features are silent spec violations.

blueprint_refs: "Calibrate ... on the 2021 training period" and "Validate on holdout dates (2021 only)"

evidence.metrics: best_objective_on_training=2.8889 but validation objective=5.5826; validation_set only 11 days for 1 user

reflection.error_identification: Large generalization gap: parameters that look good on training subsample do poorly on validation; evaluation is noisy due to tiny validation set.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Calibration uses a random subsample of training days (250) and optimizes a validation-style objective on that subsample, but the final validation set is extremely small and potentially out-of-distribution (single user). This makes selected parameters brittle. Additionally, simulator rollouts during calibration include social influence dynamics (awareness) which may overfit co-presence patterns in the subsample and not transfer.
reflection.correct_approach:
After fixing validation eligibility (7 observed days vs calendar contiguity), re-run calibration with:
- Cross-validation within training (e.g., multiple subsamples) or evaluate each candidate on a small internal holdout split per user.
- Reduce/disable social influence during calibration unless you explicitly evaluate co-presence metrics (not currently in AVAILABLE_METRIC_KEYS).
- Increase calibration iterations or adopt a structured optimizer only after validation size is healthy.
reflection.key_insight: Do not trust a single small holdout; calibration should be robustified (multi-subsample / internal holdout) especially when stochastic dynamics (social influence) are enabled.

blueprint_refs: Outputs: simulated_day_strings_in_1921Y_format (no mention of review-writing agents or OpenAI calls)

evidence.metrics: runtime ~609s; unused OpenAI integration present but not called.

reflection.error_identification: The codebase includes unrelated LLM review-writing agents and OpenAI integration that are not used by the mobility simulator and are not required by the blueprint.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Extraneous classes/functions inflate complexity and can create deployment fragility (API key requirements, dependency mismatches) even if unused. While this run succeeded, it’s a spec mismatch and maintainability risk.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove unused OpenAI/review-agent code paths from this simulator module, or isolate them behind a separate optional module that is not imported/executed in the mobility pipeline.
reflection.key_insight: Keep simulation code minimal and blueprint-aligned; unrelated agent scaffolding increases failure surface without improving metrics.

reflection.error_identification: No user feedback provided, so no user-mandated blocker issues can be triaged.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: N/A (absence of feedback).
reflection.correct_approach: Add a lightweight CLI/report that surfaces top failing metrics and 3 example bad trajectories per metric to solicit targeted user feedback next iteration.
reflection.key_insight: When feedback is empty, auto-generate a small, structured “what to comment on” bundle (worst-days + metric deltas) to bootstrap actionable feedback.

blueprint_refs: STRICT: compute using only the resident's 2021 training subset (and strictly pre-target when generating a target day).
Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 days (t-7..t-1) within 2021 only (no future leakage)
Holdout: Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date.

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics are computed on a temporal holdout, but agent patterns are fit once from all training days (not strictly pre-target per validation day).

reflection.error_identification: Even though Motivation is split-aware, the per-user Pattern (preferences, transitions, start/stop distributions) is fit once from the entire training block and then reused for all validation targets, rather than being recomputed using only days strictly before each validation date.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In main(), agent.fit_pattern_from_training(split.train_by_user[uid], env) is called once per user. This violates the blueprint requirement that Pattern/Persona for a target day must use only pre-target history, not “future” training days that occur after some validation dates (because the split is per-user, but training still spans a range of dates; for early validation targets, later training dates are still future relative to that target if the split boundary is not aligned per target).
reflection.correct_approach: For each (user, validation_day) generation, build a per-target training slice: {d in user_train_days | d < validation_day}. Fit (or update) a lightweight per-target Pattern view (at minimum: transition_pmf, category_pref, poi_memory, start_time_hist, stop_count_hist, anchors) before calling generate_day(). Cache by (uid, cutoff_date) to keep runtime manageable.
reflection.key_insight: Temporal holdout requires temporal conditioning at generation time: any learned user statistics used for a target must be computed with a cutoff < target_day, even if those days are “in training split.”

blueprint_refs: stops-per-day distribution ... Base rate is then modulated by Motivation computed from past 7 days
policy: sample baseline number of stops ... then modulate by Motivation

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.7153, stop_count_kl=0.5127 on validation.

reflection.error_identification: Daily stop counts are materially miscalibrated: the mean is off by ~0.72 stops/day and the distribution mismatch is substantial (KL ~0.51).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: In _sample_stop_count(), the “motivation” reweighting uses log(mult)*(c/baseline_mean_stops) added to log(p). This is an ad-hoc tilt that can distort the empirical histogram and is not tied to any likelihood model; also, generate_day() breaks early when t_next>1439, which truncates days and further shifts the realized stop count distribution away from the sampled target.
reflection.correct_approach: Two-part fix: (1) Replace the stop-count tilt with a proper reweighted categorical distribution: q(c) ∝ p_emp(c) * exp(eta * (c - E[c])/std[c]) where eta is derived from stop_multiplier (and clip), so the distribution shifts without exploding tails. (2) Enforce feasibility: when nearing end-of-day, shorten dwell/travel (or allow smaller min_gap) to hit the sampled stop_count more often; only break early if t is already near 1439, and otherwise resample next dwell/travel with capped values.
reflection.key_insight: If you sample a stop budget, the simulator must actively satisfy it; otherwise timing truncation silently turns a stop-count model error into a distribution error.

blueprint_refs: distance sensitivity using POI coordinates
U(poi)=... + alpha_dist*(-distance(current,poi)) + alpha_anchor*(-distance(anchor,poi))

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=1.1780 on validation; calibrated alpha_dist=0.052 (near zero), travel_time_scale=30.05.

reflection.error_identification: Trip distance distribution still differs noticeably (Wasserstein ~1.18 km) and calibration converged to almost no distance penalty, which is a red flag that the objective can be improved by “teleporting” behavior compensated by high travel_time_scale.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Two interacting causes: (1) RandomSearchCalibrator can pick alpha_dist near 0 and still do well on other metrics; there is no regularization or constraint encouraging realistic spatial choices. (2) POIEnvironment.candidates_for_category() expands a coarse category into a union of many fine categories, increasing spatial spread; with low alpha_dist and high noise, selection becomes weakly spatially grounded.
reflection.correct_approach: Add a spatial regularizer to calibration objective (still using existing metric): increase weight on trip_distance_wasserstein or include a soft prior penalty term inside the calibrator objective for alpha_dist being too small (e.g., +lambda*(max(0,1-alpha_dist))^2) computed in code where objective is assembled. Also tighten candidate selection: when a coarse category is requested, prefer fine categories proportional to the agent’s observed fine-category frequencies within that coarse bucket (derivable from train_days already) instead of uniform union expansion.
reflection.key_insight: If the optimizer drives a core behavioral weight (alpha_dist) to ~0, it’s usually exploiting a missing constraint; fix the objective or candidate set so spatial realism is necessary for a good score.

blueprint_refs: dwell-time / inter-event-time model
model uses category-specific dwell plus distance-based travel proxy

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0395; stop_count metrics are also off, consistent with timing model mismatch.

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day distribution is decent but not tight (JSD ~0.04), and the simulator can truncate days or compress schedules due to how dwell/travel are modeled from the same observed gaps.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _compute_dwell_means_by_category() uses observed inter-visit gaps as dwell estimates, but those gaps include both dwell and travel. Then _travel_minutes() adds additional travel time based on distance, double-counting travel on average and forcing earlier truncation or different time spacing than observed.
reflection.correct_approach: Debias dwell estimation using distance: when computing dwell means, subtract an estimated travel proxy from each gap using the same travel_time_scale (or a default) and POI distances, clamped to a minimum dwell. This can be done without new files/APIs because env.distance_km() is available and train trajectories already contain POIs.
reflection.key_insight: Never fit dwell directly from inter-event gaps if you also add travel later; either fit dwell on travel-adjusted gaps or fit a joint model.

blueprint_refs: Calibrate ... by maximizing likelihood ... or minimizing multi-metric discrepancy on the 2021 training period
aggregate with confidence intervals via bootstrap

evidence.metrics: Calibration selects best objective on a 250-day subsample; runtime ~299s, no bootstrap CIs reported.

reflection.error_identification: Calibration is noisy and may not generalize: it optimizes a weighted sum of “validation-style” discrepancies on a small random subsample of training days without uncertainty estimates.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator.fit() evaluates 25 random parameter sets on a fixed 250-day subsample and picks the minimum objective. This can overfit that subsample and provides no confidence bounds; blueprint expects likelihood or discrepancy minimization over training with validation reporting and bootstrap CIs.
reflection.correct_approach: Add (1) multiple subsamples per iteration (e.g., 3 shards of 250 days) and average objective, and (2) bootstrap CI computation in evaluation_results_on_validation (resample days/users and recompute metrics). Both can be implemented inside existing Evaluator/fit loops without new dependencies.
reflection.key_insight: If calibration uses stochastic subsampling, stabilize selection with repeated subsamples and report uncertainty; otherwise “best params” often reflect sampling noise, not better behavior.

blueprint_refs: objective: Generate realistic daily mobility trajectories ...
OUTPUT FORMAT STRICT ... no extra text/JSON/markdown.

evidence.metrics: Execution succeeded, but unrelated LLM review-writing components exist in the same module.

reflection.error_identification: The code includes unrelated product-review LLM agents and OpenAI SDK wiring, which is outside the mobility simulator blueprint and increases risk (dependency errors, policy leakage, longer load time).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Top-of-file classes/functions (MemoryAgent/PlanningAgent/ReviewAuthor/call_gpt5_with_responses_api) are unused by the mobility pipeline; they are leftover scaffolding and not part of the specified system.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove these unused classes/functions from the module (or gate them behind an explicit flag and avoid importing OpenAI unless needed). Keep the mobility simulator self-contained to reduce failure surface and comply with blueprint scope.
reflection.key_insight: Keep the generated codebase minimal and task-scoped; unused subsystems are latent failure points even if they don’t execute.

blueprint_refs: Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 days (t-7..t-1) within 2021 only.
Only keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days within the 2021 subset (d-7..d-1) available

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.0088; tod_jsd_avg=0.0697

reflection.error_identification: Motivation and validation eligibility use “any 7 prior observed days”, not the required (t-7..t-1) window; this can distort day-specific conditioning and hurt stop/time-of-day fit.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: holdout_split() checks len(prior_dates) >= 7 regardless of whether those dates are the literal previous 7 calendar days. _last_k_observed_days() likewise picks the last 7 observed dates, not the calendar window t-7..t-1. This violates the blueprint’s strict window semantics.
reflection.correct_approach: Implement calendar-window logic: for a target day d, require that for each delta in 1..7, a trajectory exists for (d - delta). In holdout_split(), only keep validation targets meeting this contiguity requirement. In _last_k_observed_days(), construct [hist[d-7],...,hist[d-1]] and return [] if any is missing.
reflection.key_insight: When a spec names an explicit temporal window (t-7..t-1), treat it as calendar arithmetic, not “last N samples”; enforce the same window in both feature computation and split eligibility.

blueprint_refs: stop_count_kl: divergence between simulated and ground-truth distributions of daily stop counts

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.0088; stop_count_kl=0.1396

reflection.error_identification: Stop-count KL is computed on support=1..max_count, making 0-stop days impossible in the KL comparison (even if they exist in ground truth).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator.compute_metrics() builds support = list(range(1, max_count + 1)). If ground truth contains 0-stop days (possible after filtering or parsing failures upstream, or if logs include empty), the metric definition is violated and KL can be biased.
reflection.correct_approach: Change support to range(0, max_count+1). Keep smoothing eps and ensure histograms include 0. If you believe 0-stop days should be excluded by design, explicitly document and enforce it by filtering them out consistently in both simulated and ground truth before metric computation.
reflection.key_insight: Metric implementations must match the metric definition’s support; silent support truncation is a hidden evaluation bug.

blueprint_refs: sample dwell time and add travel-time proxy based on distance; produce next_time
dwell-time / inter-event-time model
return-home hazard parameters

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.0088; tod_jsd_avg=0.0697

reflection.error_identification: Validation shows about +/−1 stop/day mean error and moderate ToD divergence, indicating the generator’s inter-event timing and day-end/return-home dynamics are not matching observed pacing.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: generate_day() sets the first visit time equal to start_time but then enforces feasibility by compressing gaps using max_gap derived from remaining time/remaining stops. This can distort ToD spacing and truncate later stops, shifting arrival-time distribution. Additionally, return-home logic only triggers on the last planned stop and only when explicit Home appears; there is no general end-of-day/return-home hazard applied throughout the day as described in the blueprint.
reflection.correct_approach: Add an explicit probabilistic termination/return-home decision at each step based on (current time, fatigue proxy, motivation-modulated return_home params) rather than only on the last stop. Separate dwell vs travel vs scheduling: sample dwell/travel, but if infeasible, resample stop_count downward (or re-plan) instead of aggressively compressing all gaps. Calibrate a last-arrival-time distribution target (even if not a formal metric) and use it to tune return_home_bias and dwell/travel parameters.
reflection.key_insight: Feasibility hacks (gap compression) improve completion but can systematically break time-of-day distributions; prefer re-planning (adjust stop budget/end day) over compressing dynamics.

blueprint_refs: distance sensitivity using POI coordinates
trip distance distributions ... Wasserstein distance

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.7842; calibrated alpha_dist=0.079 (near 0), travel_time_scale=32.99

reflection.error_identification: Calibration selected near-zero alpha_dist despite a distance metric being part of the objective; this suggests the objective allows teleporting-like behavior to fit other metrics, potentially harming distance realism.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator.fit() uses a very small soft prior: spatial_prior = 0.15*(max(0,1-alpha_dist)^2), which is insufficient when other metrics dominate. Additionally, candidate selection uses anchor-nearest-K, so with very small alpha_dist the simulator can still jump around within that candidate pool without strong penalty.
reflection.correct_approach: Strengthen spatial regularization and/or constrain alpha_dist lower bound during proposal (e.g., sample alpha_dist from [0.5,10] in _propose_params) if the intent is to prevent teleporting. Alternatively, increase the objective weight on trip_distance_wasserstein (already 0.7) or incorporate an additional penalty based on simulated mean trip distance vs ground truth (derived from existing distance lists already computed in Evaluator._trip_distances).
reflection.key_insight: If calibration repeatedly picks degenerate parameter extremes, treat it as an evaluation-signal: either the objective weights are mis-scaled or the search space needs constraints/priors.

blueprint_refs: time_of_day_category_profile (global or per-coarse-category)
category transition model
Sequential discrete-choice ... mixture of transition matrix and time-of-day propensity

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0697; transition_divergence=0.0161

reflection.error_identification: Transitions match well (low divergence) but time-of-day fit is still moderate, suggesting the ToD profile is not sufficiently individualized or conditioned.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _compute_global_time_profile() builds a global (population) ToD->coarse-category distribution. _choose_next_category() mixes user transition PMF with this global profile, but does not incorporate per-user ToD preferences or day-of-week/month modulation beyond a single “Office” weekend downweight. This under-uses blueprint conditioning signals and can miss user-specific timing patterns.
reflection.correct_approach: Extend ResidentPattern to include per-user ToD category histogram (coarse) derived from pre-target training; use it as an additional component in _choose_next_category() (e.g., a 3-way mixture: transitions vs user-ToD vs global-ToD). Add explicit day_of_week/month modifiers by learning separate profiles (weekday/weekend) from training rather than hardcoding Office-only behavior.
reflection.key_insight: Good transition fit doesn’t imply good time-of-day fit; timing usually needs user-conditional (and weekday/seasonal) profiles, not only global priors.

blueprint_refs: Link POI ids to coordinates ... by matching the exact 'Category#id' string.

evidence.metrics: stdout shows dropped_visits_not_in_catalog=12121; dropped_days_empty_after_filter=11

reflection.error_identification: A large number of observed visits are dropped because POI tokens aren’t found in the POI catalog, which can bias learned patterns and evaluation ground truth.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _filter_to_poi_catalog() removes visits not present in poi_catalog using exact token match. If 1921Y.json contains tokens with formatting differences (case, whitespace, synonyms) or categories not present in the POI file, many visits are silently removed, changing distributions used for training and validation.
reflection.correct_approach: Add diagnostics before dropping: tally top missing poi_tokens and missing fine categories. If differences are purely whitespace/case, normalize tokens during parsing (strip already done; consider case normalization only if catalog is consistent). If POI catalog is incomplete by design, consider excluding affected users/days from training/validation consistently (rather than partially dropping visits within a day), since partial-day truncation changes stop counts and transitions.
reflection.key_insight: Heavy join-drop rates indicate a data-contract mismatch; always report the most common missing keys and decide whether to normalize, augment mapping, or exclude affected samples consistently.

blueprint_refs: Set RNG seed for reproducibility.

evidence.metrics: No runtime errors; potential reproducibility risk (not directly measured).

reflection.error_identification: Reproducibility can be compromised by mutable agent state (category_awareness) interacting with per-day ordering and cached patterns; small ordering changes could alter results despite fixed seeds.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Agents maintain category_awareness across days; rollout ordering is deterministic by (date, user_id), but any future parallelization or different iteration order will change awareness updates. Pattern caching uses deepcopy into cache but assigns agent.pattern directly; this is fine, yet mixing shared mutable state with simulation order is fragile for reproducibility claims.
reflection.correct_approach: For strict reproducibility, keep social awareness in a per-rollout local structure keyed by (uid, day) or reset it at rollout start unless explicitly modeling multi-day social carryover. Document that enabling social makes trajectories order-dependent and ensure ordering is fixed (already sorted) and tested.
reflection.key_insight: If you claim seeded reproducibility, isolate mutable cross-day state or fully specify and lock the rollout ordering (and avoid hidden dependence on dict iteration/parallel scheduling).

blueprint_refs: validation_range: 'ONLY keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days within the 2021 subset (d-7..d-1) available'
comparison_method: 'compute each required metric on simulated vs 2021 validation sets, aggregate with confidence intervals via bootstrap'

evidence.metrics: Validation_set: num_users=1, num_user_days=7; stdout: 'val_days=7 excluded_val_missing_context=682'

reflection.error_identification: Metrics are computed on only 7 validation days from 1 user, making reported divergences unstable/unrepresentative and extremely sensitive to one user’s behavior.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The pipeline enforces a literal contiguous calendar window (d-7..d-1). With sparse daily logs, most candidate validation dates fail eligibility, collapsing the validation set. This is allowed by the blueprint’s strict rule, but it undermines metric reliability and calibration signal.
reflection.correct_approach: Keep the blueprint rule, but change evaluation protocol to avoid misleading metrics when eligibility collapses: (1) emit a hard warning and skip metric reporting (or mark as invalid) if validation_set.num_users or num_user_days is below a minimum threshold; (2) optionally adjust splitting so the latest 20% is taken after filtering to users with sufficiently dense 2021 coverage (still respecting strict per-user temporal separation).
reflection.key_insight: When a spec introduces strict temporal context requirements, add guardrails that detect evaluation-set collapse and prevent over-interpreting metrics from tiny samples.

blueprint_refs: stop_count_kl: 'KL divergence between simulated and ground-truth distributions of daily stop counts on the 2021 validation target set (with smoothing)'

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.2857 but stop_count_kl=3.9547 on validation (7 days)

reflection.error_identification: Despite a small mean stop-count error, the KL over stop-count distributions is extremely high (3.95), indicating the distributional shape is mismatched or the estimate is unstable.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator uses support 0..max_count from both sets with a fixed eps=1e-6. With only 7 validation days, any stop-count value present in one set but absent in the other yields near-zero mass and large KL. This is mathematically correct but becomes a noisy signal under tiny sample sizes.
reflection.correct_approach: Make KL estimation robust for small validation samples: (1) increase smoothing mass proportional to sample size (e.g., eps = 1.0 / (N + support_size)) using only existing code structure; (2) report both directions (symmetrized KL or JSD) as an additional diagnostic in calibration_log (keep required metric as-is, but add diagnostic field); (3) if validation N is below a threshold, down-weight stop_count_kl in objective_weights or skip using it for model selection.
reflection.key_insight: Distributional metrics (KL) need sample-size-aware smoothing/weighting; otherwise they dominate objectives due to estimation noise, not true behavioral mismatch.

blueprint_refs: exogenous_signals.day_of_week: 'Modulates category time-of-day propensity and stops-per-day distribution'
exogenous_signals.seasonality_month: 'Optional modulation of category propensity and activity timing'

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.4423 (high); transition_divergence=0.2614 (moderate); simulator only applies a weak weekend Office down-weight.

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day distribution divergence is very high, suggesting category timing is not matching observed daily rhythms.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Blueprint requires day-of-week/month signals to modulate time-of-day category propensity and stop counts. Code computes exogenous signals but does not learn or apply weekday/month-conditioned ToD profiles; it only reduces Office probability on weekends, leaving most ToD structure unconditioned.
reflection.correct_approach: Extend existing profile machinery without new external APIs: (1) compute separate global (and optionally per-user) time_of_day_category_profile for weekday vs weekend (and optionally month buckets) using training data; (2) in _choose_next_category, select/mix the correct profile based on exo['day_of_week'] and exo['month']; (3) similarly modulate stop_count sampling by maintaining separate stop_count_hist (weekday/weekend) in ResidentPattern (or apply a multiplicative weekend factor derived from training).
reflection.key_insight: If the spec lists exogenous signals as modulators, bake them into learned conditional distributions (weekday/weekend is the highest ROI) instead of ad-hoc single-category tweaks.

blueprint_refs: policy_form: '(1) sample baseline number of stops ... from learned per-user distributions (Pattern). ... Motivation modulates stop count ...'
execution: 'sequential decision process over a day ... dwell-time / inter-event-time model ... end day / return home hazard'

evidence.metrics: stop_count_kl=3.9547 while stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.2857; code caps stop_count using estimated gap feasibility and may early-break if remaining stops cannot fit.

reflection.error_identification: Mean stop count is close, but the distribution is far off, consistent with truncation/censoring effects (capping and early stopping) that preserve averages but distort shape.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: After sampling stop_count from the user histogram, generate_day caps it with a heuristic feasibility bound and may terminate early if time would overflow. This introduces a non-data-derived censoring mechanism not described in the blueprint and can systematically distort the stop-count distribution.
reflection.correct_approach: Replace hard capping with model-consistent mechanisms: (1) incorporate a calibrated end-of-day hazard earlier (already present) and remove/relax the deterministic feasibility cap; (2) if you keep a cap, compute it per-agent using that agent’s start_time_hist and dwell_time_mean_by_category (not global mean) to reduce systematic bias; (3) log the fraction of days where capping/early-break happens to quantify distortion and tune parameters accordingly.
reflection.key_insight: Avoid ‘feasibility hacks’ that post-edit sampled behavioral counts; model feasibility should emerge from dwell/travel/hazard dynamics, not deterministic truncation.

blueprint_refs: construction_from_data: 'Link POI ids to coordinates using poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json by matching the exact Category#id string.'

evidence.metrics: stdout: 'dropped_visits=12121 dropped_days=11' out of parsed_ok_2021=3339 day records; large POI token mismatch indicates join/key issues or inconsistent token formatting.

reflection.error_identification: A very large number of visits are dropped because POI tokens are not found in the POI catalog, which can change user patterns, category shares, and POI frequency rankings used for recall.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _filter_to_poi_catalog performs strict token membership filtering (exact string match). If 1921Y.json contains tokens with inconsistent capitalization/spacing or slightly different naming than the POI catalog, the join will drop substantial data, biasing learned patterns and evaluation targets.
reflection.correct_approach: Add conservative normalization using only existing parsing utilities: (1) when parsing poi_token in parse_day_record, apply consistent stripping and collapse internal whitespace; (2) optionally attempt an exact-match after casefolding both catalog keys and parsed tokens if the dataset is known to vary in casing (build a lookup map from normalized->canonical token from poi_catalog); (3) log top-N most-dropped poi tokens to pinpoint systematic mismatches.
reflection.key_insight: Exact-key joins in behavioral simulation pipelines should always include diagnostics on dropped keys and a minimal, well-defined normalization step before discarding data.

blueprint_refs: policy_form: 'choose a concrete POI within that category by softmax utility ... alpha_pref ... alpha_dist ...'
parameters: 'alpha_pref ... affects POI repetition and topk_poi_recall'

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.4444; calibrated alpha_dist=5.02, exploration_rate=0.1469; candidates_for_category uses K=100 with anchor-nearest ordering, which can exclude many frequent POIs if they are not near anchor/current.

reflection.error_identification: Recall of frequent POIs is low, suggesting the simulator often fails to include the globally frequent POIs from ground truth in its generated set.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _choose_next_poi restricts candidates to a small pool (K=100) biased toward nearest-to-anchor, then applies a substantial distance penalty. This can prevent selecting historically frequent but farther POIs (especially if memory top-K is small or doesn’t cover the validation top-K set after filtering).
reflection.correct_approach: Improve coverage of frequent POIs without breaking distance realism: (1) increase DEFAULT_CANDIDATE_POOL_SIZE (e.g., 300) or include an explicit union with the agent’s top memory POIs regardless of distance (already partially done, but ensure it includes top-K across all categories, not only the chosen category); (2) change candidate construction to mix nearest-to-anchor with a random sample from the full category pool (e.g., 70% nearest, 30% random) using existing RNG; (3) during calibration, allow alpha_dist to be smaller if it improves recall but add a separate penalty based on trip_distance_wasserstein rather than hard lower-bounding alpha_dist proposals.
reflection.key_insight: If a choice model uses both candidate pruning and distance penalties, recall metrics often fail due to candidate-set bias; diversify candidates before tuning utility weights.

evidence.metrics: No user feedback provided; proceeding with blueprint/metrics-driven diagnosis.

reflection.error_identification: No explicit user-reported failures; remaining issues are inferred from blueprint mismatches and observed metrics/logs.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: N/A (triage placeholder).
reflection.correct_approach: N/A.
reflection.key_insight: When user feedback is absent, treat blueprint constraints + metric pathologies + logs as the prioritization signal.

blueprint_refs: “Link POI ids to coordinates using poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json by matching the exact 'Category#id' string.”
“Build POI universe with lat/lon per POI id …”

evidence.metrics: stdout: dropped_visits_not_in_catalog=12120; top dropped tokens include 'Forest#161', 'Platform#156', 'Construction and Landscaping#2', etc.

reflection.error_identification: A large fraction of observed visits are being discarded before fitting/simulation (12,120 dropped visits; 11 dropped days). This likely distorts learned preferences/transitions and reduces overlap with validation POIs, contributing to low POI recall and distribution mismatches.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The pipeline hard-filters trajectories to only POIs present in the POI catalog (_filter_to_poi_catalog). If the POI file does not include all tokens present in 1921Y.json (common when categories differ or naming differs), this removal changes the empirical distributions used for Pattern/Persona and ToD/transition modeling. The blueprint requires joining by exact token, but does not require discarding unmatched visits; discarding is a designed choice that can harm fidelity.
reflection.correct_approach: Stop discarding unmatched visits entirely; instead: (1) keep the original POI token for modeling categories/stop counts/transitions, (2) mark coordinates as missing for those tokens and exclude only those pairs from distance-based computations (trip distances, travel-time proxy), (3) in POI selection, allow sampling from user memory even if coordinates are missing but downweight distance terms when coords unavailable. Concretely: remove/relax _filter_to_poi_catalog so it does not drop visits/days; modify POIEnvironment.has_coords/coords/distance_km usage to handle missing coords gracefully (already partially done), and adjust Evaluator._trip_distances meta reporting (already reports match rate).
reflection.key_insight: Never delete observed events to satisfy a partial join; carry them forward with missing-feature flags, and only skip the specific computations that require those features.

blueprint_refs: “Only keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days within the 2021 subset (d-7..d-1) available for Motivation computation; otherwise exclude …”

evidence.metrics: stdout: train_days=2639 val_days=7 excluded_val_missing_context=682; evaluation meta: num_users=1, num_days=7; warnings about small evaluation set.

reflection.error_identification: Validation collapses to 7 days from 1 user, making all reported validation metrics unstable/unrepresentative (and potentially explaining extreme divergences like tod_jsd_avg=0.412 and stop_count_kl=0.911).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: holdout_split enforces a strict calendar-contiguous requirement: each validation day d must have observations present for every day d-1..d-7 (not just 7 prior observed days). This is stronger than many real datasets can satisfy (missing days are common), and it dramatically reduces evaluation coverage. Although the blueprint wording uses (d-7..d-1), it is ambiguous whether contiguity is required; the current implementation requires contiguity, causing evaluation set collapse.
reflection.correct_approach: Relax eligibility to “at least 7 prior observed days before target_day within 2021” rather than “observed on each of the prior 7 calendar days”. Implement by collecting the most recent 7 observed days with date < d (sorted) and using those for Motivation. Update both holdout_split (eligibility) and MobilitySimulator._last_k_observed_days (motivation history retrieval) accordingly. Keep the strict no-future-leakage rule (only days < d).
reflection.key_insight: Eligibility rules should match data sparsity: prefer ‘k most recent observations’ over ‘k consecutive calendar days’ unless the dataset is truly daily-complete.

blueprint_refs: “Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 days (t-7..t-1) within 2021 only.”
“Validate … only targets with >=7 prior days available”

evidence.metrics: stdout shows very few eligible validation days; motivation returns neutral when last7 not available.

reflection.error_identification: When the 7-day context is missing, Motivation becomes a fixed neutral vector, reducing day-specific adaptation; with sparse data this can be frequent and can degrade stop-count and timing fit.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: _last_k_observed_days requires all 7 specific prior calendar days to exist in history; otherwise it returns []. This makes Motivation unavailable even when there are plenty of prior observations but with gaps. This interacts with the strict eligibility filter, but also affects any generation run where history is sparse.
reflection.correct_approach: Change _last_k_observed_days to fetch the last k observed trajectories strictly before target_day (within 2021), ignoring gaps. If fewer than k exist, either (a) exclude the target day from evaluation (for strict validation) or (b) compute Motivation from the available window with shrinkage to neutral (for generation robustness). Also add lightweight diagnostics: count how often Motivation is neutral due to insufficient history.
reflection.key_insight: Conditioning features should degrade gracefully under missingness; strict gating should live in evaluation selection, not inside the feature computation primitive.

blueprint_refs: “time_of_day_category_profile … normalized per time bin; initialized from empirical category counts by time-of-day from 2021 training data; fine-tuned globally”

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.412 (high); simulated validation examples show many mid-day starts and some late-night starts (e.g., 21:00, 23:14) with small sample.

reflection.error_identification: Time-of-day distribution mismatch is large (JSD ~0.41), indicating the simulator’s arrival-time histogram differs substantially from ground truth on validation.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Global and user ToD profiles are sparse dicts over bins and categories without smoothing/backoff for missing bins. When a bin has no profile (common), _choose_next_category falls back to transitions/preferences which are not ToD-conditioned, while timing is primarily driven by sampled dwell/travel/gap heuristics rather than learned inter-event timing by ToD. The blueprint expects ToD profiles to be core drivers; current implementation uses them only for category choice, not for time increments, and has no smoothing to ensure bin coverage.
reflection.correct_approach: Add Laplace/Dirichlet smoothing when building per-bin profiles so every bin has a non-empty distribution (e.g., mix each bin with global category prior). Additionally, condition gap/dwell on ToD or learn an empirical inter-arrival distribution (by user or global) from training and sample gaps from it, rather than relying on dwell_mean + travel_proxy + small jitter. This keeps timestamps aligned with observed rhythms and should reduce tod_jsd_avg.
reflection.key_insight: If a metric is about time, you need a learned time-generative component (gap/dwell/inter-arrival); using ToD only to pick categories rarely fixes ToD histogram divergence.

blueprint_refs: “stops-per-day distribution … Base rate is then modulated by Motivation …”

evidence.metrics: stop_count_kl=0.91096 (high), stop_count_abs_mean_error=0.4286; stdout: days_stopcount_truncated=363.

reflection.error_identification: Daily stop-count distribution diverges strongly from ground truth (KL ~0.91) and the simulator frequently truncates sampled stop counts due to infeasible time budget (363 truncations logged).
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Stop count is sampled from the empirical histogram, then truncated based on a crude feasibility estimate derived from _estimated_gap_minutes_agent. This truncation changes the realized stop-count distribution in a way not accounted for in the sampling model (i.e., it’s an implicit censoring). Additionally, the gap model is heuristic and can be inconsistent with sampled dwell/travel, making truncation frequent.
reflection.correct_approach: Replace post-hoc truncation with a feasibility-aware stop-count model: compute a per-user feasible max stop count for the day based on a learned inter-arrival/gap distribution (or observed day duration), then sample stop count directly from the empirical histogram conditioned on <= max_feasible (renormalize) without a second re-sample path. Also log the distribution of max_feasible and compare to sampled counts to detect systematic censoring.
reflection.key_insight: Avoid ‘sample then clip’ for discrete distributions when evaluating distribution metrics; instead sample from the correctly truncated/conditioned distribution.

blueprint_refs: “routine-vs-exploration mixing … exploration_rate … choose a concrete POI …”

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.3333 (low); best_params show alpha_pref=9.06 high and exploration_rate=0.083 low.

reflection.error_identification: The simulator covers only ~33% of ground-truth top-50 POIs on validation.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: Calibration on a training subsample can push alpha_pref very high and exploration_rate low because it optimizes a weighted objective on training-like statistics; combined with candidate selection biased toward anchor-near POIs, this can reduce diversity and prevent coverage of less frequent (but top-K globally) POIs. Additionally, earlier POI-catalog filtering may have altered what counts as top-K and what remains sampleable.
reflection.correct_approach: During calibration, add an explicit regularizer/constraint to keep exploration_rate above a floor (e.g., >=0.15) or include an auxiliary term that rewards POI support coverage. In _choose_next_poi, incorporate a small probability mass for sampling from category-wide candidates even when exploiting memory (e.g., epsilon-greedy within-category). Also ensure POI universe includes unmatched tokens if you apply the ‘don’t drop visits’ fix.
reflection.key_insight: Optimizers will overfit to repeat-preference unless exploration has either (a) a floor, or (b) a metric/regularizer that values support coverage.

blueprint_refs: “Calibrate … on the 2021 training period … Validate on holdout dates … report metrics.”

evidence.metrics: Training-subsample objectives are low with strong metrics on subsample, but validation metrics are much worse (e.g., tod_jsd_avg 0.05 on train subsample vs 0.41 on validation).

reflection.error_identification: Calibration appears to fit a training subsample well but does not generalize to validation (large metric gaps), suggesting objective/selection mismatch and/or unstable validation set.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator optimizes on a random subsample of training days (budget=250) and never evaluates on the true validation split during calibration. With a tiny validation set (1 user), generalization can look poor. Also, the calibration rollout uses the same train_by_user as simulator history, making pattern fitting and evaluation closely coupled to the sampled days, amplifying overfit.
reflection.correct_approach: During calibration, periodically evaluate candidate parameters on the validation split (even if small) and select best by a combined criterion. Alternatively, use cross-validation within training (e.g., per-user last 10% of training as internal validation) to prevent overfit. Ensure the simulator history for generation of evaluation days is strictly pre-target (already done via set_history_by_user + target dates), but avoid reusing the same sampled days as both history and targets within the subsample when computing training objectives (evaluate on targets, condition on earlier days only).
reflection.key_insight: If you can’t afford full training-likelihood calibration, at least include an out-of-sample checkpoint loop; otherwise random search will exploit quirks of the sampled objective.

blueprint_refs: Outputs and roles are mobility-specific; no product-review agent is specified.

evidence.metrics: Not directly reflected in metrics; increases code surface and potential dependency failures (OpenAI key required if invoked).

reflection.error_identification: Non-mobility LLM 'review writing' scaffolding is included in the codebase, unrelated to the blueprint’s mobility simulation task.
reflection.root_cause_analysis: The module defines LLM agents for product review generation and OpenAI API calls, which are unused by the mobility pipeline but add unnecessary dependencies and risk (e.g., API key errors if accidentally invoked). This is a spec mismatch and maintainability issue.
reflection.correct_approach: Remove the review-related classes and OpenAI call helpers from this mobility simulator entrypoint, or isolate them behind a separate module/file not imported/executed by default. Keep the mobility pipeline minimal and deterministic.
reflection.key_insight: Avoid bundling unrelated agent scaffolds into a simulation runtime; keep the executable surface focused to reduce accidental failures and simplify evaluation.

When a metric is used as a calibration signal, implement it exactly per definition; a subtly mis-specified denominator/truncation can collapse the signal (here: always near 0), making calibration chase the wrong objective.
Never let the declared format_spec diverge from emitted strings; keep a single canonical serializer and have parsers accept supersets for robustness.
Calibration optimizes exactly what you measure; if your metric suite drifts from spec, you’ll tune the wrong behavior even if the simulator ‘looks good’ on internal metrics.

When a divergence metric saturates at a constant across parameter sweeps, treat it as a taxonomy/support mismatch or a collapsed feature extractor; add overlap diagnostics and ensure identical representation for GT vs SIM.
If GT-derived trajectory stats (like step counts) are zero, debug parsing/joining before tuning the generator; otherwise all spatial metrics become meaningless.
When outputs are consumed by external evaluators, treat key names as API contracts; add backward-compatible aliases rather than renaming fields outright.

When the evaluation requires exact POI token matches, any purely “user-history-only” POI preference will collapse recall under distribution shift; always include global/popularity smoothing and candidate-set pruning.
Don’t silently substitute evaluation metrics: implement blueprint metrics directly, and only add extras as explicitly non-contract diagnostics.
For circular/ordered domains like time-of-day, prefer distance-aware metrics (EMD/Wasserstein) over divergence on coarse bins when the spec demands shift magnitude.
Match the metric family in the spec: MAE on shares and JSD capture different failures; using the wrong one can miscalibrate the shift parameters.

If you sample a target sequence length but later time-feasibility truncates the sequence, you must either enforce feasibility during length sampling or adjust gaps to honor the sampled length; otherwise stop-count metrics will be systematically biased.
Distance-based metrics are only as good as the POI-token join; always implement robust normalization and log unmatched identifiers before trusting mobility-radius results.
Context windows in OOD simulations must be time-bounded even in fallback paths; otherwise you silently change the conditioning regime and may inflate/deflate metrics unpredictably.
When output filenames are fixed by contract, make the evaluated split unambiguous in the JSON payload to prevent accidental ‘validation vs test’ misuse downstream.
Treat metric names as API: if the spec lists metrics, compute them explicitly with exact names and avoid burying them only in diagnostics.
If the evaluation includes exact-POI overlap, POI sampling must be conditioned (category → POI) and should include a high-mass component on the user’s frequent POIs within that category.
To match count MAE and count KL simultaneously, use a principled exponential tilt/temperature transform of the baseline PMF rather than shifting only the mean with arbitrary penalties.

Coarse start-time bins plus unconditioned gaps usually produce correct-looking averages but fail distributional metrics; add conditioning on stop index/transition to recover realistic temporal structure.
When a spec says “JSD”, using MAE instead can hide mode-collapse/mass-shift errors; match the divergence family required by the evaluation.
Spatial metrics are only meaningful when coordinate coverage is comparable; normalize tokens early and compute distance metrics on matched-coordinate subsets.
If a spec provides an online context window, use it to condition multiple parts of generation (counts, categories, POIs), not just the starting location.

If the blueprint allows an optional aggregation (super-category), implement both and clearly report which one drives selection; otherwise you risk optimizing an easier proxy metric that hides failures.
A context window should influence more than the initial state; use it to bias the full choice set toward recently seen user-specific POIs to improve recall under regime shifts.
Stop count, gaps, and day time budget must be jointly consistent; sampling them independently and then clipping times tends to inflate count errors and distort temporal statistics.
Avoid post-hoc rejection rules on learned temporal distributions; encode constraints directly into the fitted distribution or you will create distribution shifts that show up as TOD divergence.
If a metric depends on external joins, missingness can dominate; measure and equalize join success across simulated and GT before trusting distribution distances.
Don’t silently change calibrated parameter bounds; calibration depends on the search space matching the spec.

If the evaluation target is at POI token granularity, generating only higher-level categories will often look good on aggregate mix metrics while failing identity/recall metrics; model the finest target level explicitly.

Any feature derived from the labeled/evaluation-period ground truth (even if temporally earlier) must be treated as privileged information unless the task explicitly provides it as observed input at inference time.

Matching only the mean of a discrete behavioral distribution is often insufficient under regime shift; add at least one parameter controlling dispersion/tail weight and validate the realized histogram after all feasibility constraints.
Recall@K for sets is hurt by within-day repetition; enforce diversity or diminishing returns when sampling discrete items for a fixed-length list.
Feasibility clamping after independent gap sampling often reshapes the time distribution; generate time structure in a constraint-aware way rather than fixing it post hoc.
When a metric depends on optional metadata (coords), calibration becomes unreliable unless you control for missingness symmetry and log coverage/eligibility explicitly.
High-dimensional calibration with noisy objectives needs either many trials or structured/staged search; small random searches mostly measure noise.
Do not swap evaluation metrics for convenient proxies: implement required metrics exactly (or emit both with clear names) to avoid optimizing the wrong objective.
Calibration objectives must match the spec’s metric definitions (not just “similar” statistics), otherwise optimization will produce good-looking numbers that don’t satisfy the actual task.
OOD context windows need deterministic, spec-compliant backfill rules; arbitrary caps create silent distribution shifts and undermine personalization.
For POI-level recall tasks, controlling candidate set size is as important as weighting—high-cardinality categories require pruning/structuring to avoid probability dilution.
If you calibrate a multiplier on a discrete outcome, ensure the base distribution has enough support; otherwise optimization can’t express the needed shift.
Metric definitions shape what the simulator learns; prefix-based recall silently encourages ordering hacks rather than true day-level overlap.
Strict split hygiene includes clear artifact naming—mislabeling validation vs test can invalidate the experimental protocol even if the code is correct.
When calibration is black-box, reduce per-trial cost with deterministic subsampling/Successive Halving; better exploration often beats minor model tweaks.
If per-day MAE is high but distribution-level KL is low, the generator is matching marginals but missing conditional structure; add conditioning/dispersion controls rather than only mean scaling.
POI recall is dominated by category correctness and candidate-set sharpness; fix the first-stop category consistency and shrink candidate pools before tuning weights.
Never hide unit conversions behind legacy metric names; calibration will optimize the wrong scale and comparisons become meaningless.
Metric-key compatibility hacks must be audited with a unit test that asserts each output key equals the intended computed quantity.
OOD context windows should be defined in calendar time first; only then backfill to handle missingness—otherwise you inadvertently condition on arbitrary long-history slices.
Always guard side-effectful entrypoints; evaluation systems often import modules and expect zero execution on import.
